channel.title: Ashwood Chronicles
channel.description: Ancient stories. Quiet hours. Sleep well, wherever you are tonight.
channel.itunes:author: Mr. Ashwood

item.title: Carl Sagan and the Cosmic Calendar
item.description:
It is 1976. Carl Sagan sits alone in his office at Cornell, past midnight, with a yellow legal pad covered in calculations. He has been working for hours on a single idea: what if the entire history of the universe were compressed into one calendar year. The Big Bang at midnight on January first. Tonight, December thirty-first, one second before midnight. Tonight, you are there.
Let the quiet of that office settle over you as you drift off. The slow narration and gentle pacing are designed to help you fall asleep, a bedtime story built for deep rest.
Binaural beats and sleep frequencies are woven into the audio.
If you would like to return to these hours — you know where to find us.

item.title: Miles Davis, Listening Back to Kind of Blue — A Sleep Story (1959)
item.description:
March 1959. Miles Davis has just recorded Kind of Blue at Columbia's 30th Street Studio in two sessions, using modal sketches instead of arrangements. The musicians have gone home, but Miles has stayed alone in the control room after midnight, asking the engineer to play back the takes one more time. Tonight, you are there.
Let yourself drift off as the reels turn in the half-light, as Bill Evans' piano floats through the speakers, as you listen to your own trumpet and feel that something has shifted — a door opened that cannot be closed. This slow narration invites you to fall asleep inside that quiet moment of recognition, before the world knew what Kind of Blue would become.
Binaural beats and sleep frequencies are woven into the audio to guide you toward deep rest.
If you would like to return to these hours — you know where to find us.

item.title: Jaco Pastorius and the Bridge at Dawn
item.description:
September 1987. Jaco Pastorius stands on a Fort Lauderdale bridge as the sky begins to pale, the music in his head playing without need of his hands, the water dark and still below. Tonight, you are there.
Let this slow narration guide you toward sleep as you walk with him in those final quiet hours, the bridge stretching in both directions, the sky lightening at the edges. Drift into deep rest as dawn arrives over the Intracoastal Waterway.
This episode features binaural beats and sleep frequencies woven into the soundscape to support natural sleep cycles.
If you would like to return to these hours — you know where to find us.

item.title: Jaco Pastorius and the Bridge at Dawn
item.description:

Let this slow narration guide you toward sleep as you walk with him in those final quiet hours, the bridge stretching in both directions, the sky lightening at the edges. Drift into deep rest as dawn arrives over the Intracoastal Waterway.
This episode features binaural beats and sleep frequencies woven into the soundscape to support natural sleep cycles.
If you would like to return to these hours — you know where to find us.

item.description:
March 1959. Miles Davis has just recorded Kind of Blue at Columbia's 30th Street Studio in two sessions, using modal sketches instead of arrangements. The musicians have gone home, but Miles has stayed alone in the control room after midnight, asking the engineer to play back the takes one more time. Tonight, you are there.
Let yourself drift off as the reels turn in the half-light, as Bill Evans' piano floats through the speakers, as you listen to your own trumpet and feel that something has shifted — a door opened that cannot be closed. This slow narration invites you to fall asleep inside that quiet moment of recognition, before the world knew what Kind of Blue would become.
Binaural beats and sleep frequencies are woven into the audio to guide you toward deep rest.
If you would like to return to these hours — you know where to find us.
